videos.title: 5 Practical Ways to Take Control of Your Life | Jim VandeHei | TED
videos.summary:
Jim VandeHei, CEO of Axios and founder of Politico, argues that the single most effective lever for building a meaningful life is to stop obsessing over what you can't control—politics, AI, circumstance, other people—and become maniacal about the variables you actually can. He traces his own path from an undistinguished college student with a 1.49 GPA and a pack-a-day smoking habit to interviewing presidents and running successful companies, identifying five concrete, free practices that anyone can start today. The talk isn't motivational theater; it's a working framework distilled from watching successful people across different fields execute relentlessly on their own terms.

Key Takeaways

  • You control today: every decision from the moment you wake up—whether you doomscroll or meditate, what you eat, how you respond when someone snaps at you—either moves you toward feeling better or worse about yourself, and nobody else makes those calls.
  • You control your reactions: the practice of "doing the next right thing" is easier than trying to be perfect; it's a decision you can make in any moment, and when practiced during hard times, it compounds into genuine character.
  • You control your reality: your inputs—podcasts, newsletters, YouTube channels, friends, the information you choose to consume—directly shape your outputs and worldview; high-quality, free information is more available now than at any point in history.
  • You control how you're seen: by imagining yourself as an observer watching yourself through other people's eyes (rather than trusting your own perception), you can intentionally shape the impression and experience you leave with others.
  • You control your destiny: rejecting the idea that you're a log floating downstream; you can choose to go upstream, against the current, and that choice is fully within your control.
  • Study people better than you: VandeHei's breakthrough came from systematically copying the habits, reading lists, vocabulary, and fitness practices of people he admired until he internalized them as his own.
  • The deathbed test: write down three things that would make you feel you lived the life you wanted, then identify the three things required to actually achieve that vision; this exercise puts you ahead of 95 percent of people in terms of intentionality.
  • When shit happens, shine: in the worst periods—including a painful exit from Politico and the adoption of a traumatized teenager—the commitment to doing the next right thing and modeling grace under pressure ripples through everyone around you in ways you don't fully realize at the time.

Deeper Dive

VandeHei's argument lands with unusual force because it refuses both the self-help cliché and the victim narrative. He doesn't say "believe in yourself" or "manifest your dreams." Instead, he describes a brutally pragmatic process: identify the variables you can actually control, stop wasting energy on the ones you can't, and then compound small decisions over time. The talk opens with a remarkable admission—his younger self was genuinely unremarkable, a 1.49 GPA student smoking a pack a day while delivering pizzas—which makes his point credible. He didn't win some genetic lottery or catch one lucky break; he became a student of other people's habits and methodically copied what worked until it became his own operating system.

The five practices are arranged in order of increasing subtlety. The first three (controlling your day, your reactions, your information diet) are straightforward and actionable—almost obvious once stated. But the fourth—controlling how you're seen—is the real craft move. It requires simultaneously holding two perspectives: your own experience and your perception of how you appear to others. VandeHei frames this as an "out-of-body experience," watching yourself through others' eyes. This isn't about manipulation; it's about recognizing that your presence shapes the environment for everyone around you, and that recognition lets you choose whether to amplify or diminish certain aspects of yourself in real time. He anchors this with a story about adopting a teenage boy while already having two teenagers at home; his deliberate choice to show love, forgiveness, and persistence during an extremely difficult period had measurable effects on how that young man experienced the crisis and, implicitly, who he became.

The final frame—imagining your life from your deathbed and working backward—is presented as a writing exercise, not a visualization trick. The specificity matters. You're not dreaming about success in the abstract; you're asking yourself what three things would need to be true for you to feel you lived your life your way, and what it takes between now and then to make that real. VandeHei's point is that this exercise is a form of ownership that most people never undertake, and that alone puts you ahead of the vast majority in terms of intentional direction.

"You control you. That became my mantra, and that became my map for life."
videos.forYou: VandeHei separates control from luck by arguing that excellence is built through compounding small, repeatable decisions in areas where you actually have agency—not willpower theater, but systematic copying of better people's habits until they become yours. The sharpest move is his reframing of presence as a craft problem: you can't control how others perceive you, but you can control the internal discipline of watching yourself through their eyes and adjusting in real time, which is a different conversation about intentionality than most motivational talks land on. Worth twenty minutes if you think about how voice and direction get built over time.

videos.title: Jeff Kinney | Diary of a Wimpy Kid: Partypooper | Talks at Google
videos.summary:
Jeff Kinney, creator of the Diary of a Wimpy Kid series—a franchise that has sold over 300 million copies across 73+ languages—sat down at Google to discuss the creative process behind his books, films, and latest release, "Partypooper." This talk moves beyond typical author interviews: Kinney walks through his actual working methods, the role of discipline and routine in generating 2,000+ jokes per book, how he thinks about visual composition to land humor, and the deeply unglamorous truth that he worked a day job for a decade after publication before committing fully to writing.

The conversation touches on everything from why he chose to write slowly and safely (rather than taking a big leap) to how he uses environments like cemeteries and airports to unlock creative output, to why cartoon characters don't age the way literary ones do, and how he's shifted from writing "an American kid" to a universal character that resonates across 42 countries. Kinney is also forthright about the machinery of his success: the role of an editor, the discipline required to keep working when you're not feeling funny, and what happens when you stop consuming and start creating.

Key Takeaways

  • Kinney has worked in a structured morning routine only recently in his life—meditation, yoga, exercise, then focused writing for roughly 2.5 hours before his "comedy muscle" tires—a shift that happened in his 50s after decades of valuing chaos, and it's made him measurably more efficient at shipping multiple screenplays and books per year.
  • He writes jokes in massive volume (now ~2,000 per book, up from 700), treats the process like a grind that produces mostly unusable material, and relies on simply highlighting what works rather than editing—the assumption being that if you generate enough output, the good ideas will surface naturally.
  • Visual composition in his books is not decorative; the picture often *delivers* the joke rather than reinforcing text, and sometimes it subverts the joke entirely, creating cognitive dissonance that's the actual source of humor—this is what he calls "the most sophisticated way to write."
  • He kept his day job as a software engineer for 10 years after Diary of a Wimpy Kid was published and successful, never took a financial risk, and only stopped working when the company was sold—a deliberate choice to play it safe rather than chase the cultural narrative of the artist who "quits everything."
  • Greg Heffley does not age or fundamentally change across 20+ books because cartoon characters operate under different rules than literary characters; they allow the world to change while the character stays constant, which is why the story never has to end (unlike Harry Potter, who must graduate and grow up).
  • His best writing environments have been unconventional and often chosen impulsively—a cemetery during COVID with Oreos and Starburst jelly beans, or an unplanned trip to Iceland after leaving his mother's house on Mother's Day—because he needs to be around other people speaking a language he doesn't understand (companionship without distraction).
  • Screenwriting operates "at the speed of thought" compared to book writing and is significantly faster and more exciting to him than prose, though both require deep focus and both have taught him that you have to write even when you're not feeling funny, using systems and routine to bypass motivation entirely.
  • Over time, he's deliberately shifted from writing about "an average American kid" to writing about universal childhood experiences (parents, teachers, bullies, homework) that work across 42 countries and 70+ languages, because the most powerful thing a book can do is let a reader see themselves reflected back.

Deeper Dive

The most striking throughline in this conversation is Kinney's relationship to constraint and discipline. He describes his childhood self as "an adult trapped in a kid's body"—an observer rather than a participant—and his fifth-grade teacher Mrs. Norton teaching him the difference between "laughing with" and "laughing at." That early feedback seems to have shaped everything: his understanding that comedy requires intention, not just performance; his later refusal to read online reviews (which would be "laughing at"); and his entire approach to craft as something that can be developed through practice and volume, not inspiration. What's unsettling about his current system is that it works *despite* having no innate love of the work itself. He says: "It's not that fun to do the work honestly. It's like hard and I'm on deadline these days." But he does it anyway, framing it as a privilege and a responsibility to the millions of kids reading his books. That's not motivation-poster language; it's the sound of someone who has built infrastructure to work independently of how he feels on any given day.

The visual composition insight deserves attention if you think about how form shapes meaning. Kinney's insistence that the picture must *deliver* the joke, not merely illustrate it, is a specific compositional choice with real constraints: if you removed the images from his books, they'd fall apart. That's different from most illustrated children's books, where the text would still work without the picture. It's also different from how screenwriting works (where you describe a scene and let animators build it), or how music works (where you have melody, harmony, rhythm operating in parallel). The constraint he's chosen—text and image in tight functional interdependence—is what makes his books distinctive. And he arrived at it by noticing that cognitive dissonance (the gap between what text says and what image shows) is where the laugh actually lives. That's a principle worth holding if you're thinking about how medium shapes what an audience feels.

Finally, there's the question of voice and scale. Kinney has spent two decades building a character who does not change, publishing in 73 languages, and has never met most of his readers. Yet he speaks repeatedly about the importance of kids seeing themselves in the pages. That tension—between a fixed character and a global, infinitely diverse audience—he's resolved partly through the choice to write about universals (school, family, social fear) and partly through iterative tweaks to imagery to respect cultural context. He's also solved it by touring constantly and watching kids perform skits of his characters, which gives him a feedback loop that doesn't rely on reading comments online. The operating system here is: write for the broadest possible human experience, ship it, tour to stay in contact with the audience, and trust that specificity (Greg's voice, his particular brand of selfish rationalization) is what actually travels across languages and cultures, not generality.

"There's a difference between laughing with and laughing at." — Mrs. Norton, fifth-grade teacher (as recounted by Kinney)
videos.forYou: Kinney talks openly about a process that's the opposite of inspiration-driven: he writes 2,000 jokes per book knowing most won't work, uses unglamorous environments (a cemetery, an airport) to unlock output, and built his entire career on the principle that you have to keep working even when you don't feel funny. The specific insight worth your time is how he uses visual composition not as decoration but as the actual delivery mechanism for humor—the picture does the joke, the text sets it up—which means understanding his books requires thinking about the interdependence of form and meaning rather than treating words and images as separate layers. If you think about craft as practice (not just output), and how form constrains what becomes possible to say, that compositional logic is sharper than most craft talks get.

videos.title: What Filmmakers Get Wrong About Distribution - Zac Reeder [FULL INTERVIEW]
videos.summary:
Zac Reeder, an Emmy-nominated producer and former Netflix executive, spent over a decade in film distribution before becoming a producer's rep and financing consultant. This interview captures his hard-earned philosophy about what independent filmmakers consistently misunderstand about getting their work distributed—and why most fail to plan for distribution until it's too late.

Reeder's central argument is structural: filmmakers operate in a vacuum, making creative decisions without understanding the economic realities of the marketplace they're about to enter. He frames this as a planning problem, not a talent problem. Most films don't fail because they're poorly made; they fail because they were made for the wrong budget, with the wrong cast configuration, or without any clear path to buyers—decisions locked in before principal photography even begins.

The interview unfolds across concrete business realities: cast as the primary distribution lever, the collapse of festival-based discovery, why Netflix isn't a realistic home for 99% of indie projects, and how to actually navigate film markets and secure representation. Reeder speaks from acquisitions experience (he greenlit films for Hallmark and MarVista for a decade) and sales experience, which gives him unusual clarity about what buyers actually respond to versus what filmmakers hope they will.

Key Takeaways

  • The optimal time to bring a producer's rep into a project is during treatment or pre-production—when major creative and budget decisions are still malleable—not after the film is finished and those decisions are locked in.
  • Cast is the primary distribution lever; buyers' first question is always "who's in it?" and by "cast" they mean recognizable names that people can identify without explanation (not "the third lead on a Hulu show").
  • Most filmmakers reference Blair Witch and Napoleon Dynamite as distribution models, but they were statistical outliers among thousands of films released in their era; relying on festival discovery or betting on Netflix pickup is unrealistic planning.
  • Distribution planning should be a core business question from day one (like it is in clothing or food), not an afterthought; the creative half of filmmaking is only half the journey—getting it to an audience is the other half.
  • Filmmakers outside the industry often bring outdated distribution models to their projects and can be resistant to honest assessment if they've succeeded in other business domains, mistakenly believing film follows the same rules.
  • Film markets (Berlin, Cannes, American Film Market) are intelligence-gathering spaces where you learn what's actually selling, meet buyers face-to-face, and build relationships—but should be approached strategically (late in the market when sellers are less busy) and with respect for participants' time.
  • The data about real film sales is largely invisible to independent filmmakers because, unlike stock markets, there's no public record of what each film actually sold for or what distribution it secured; this information asymmetry drives unrealistic expectations.
  • Reeder turns down projects he doesn't believe in regardless of payment, because his credibility as a salesperson depends on buyers trusting that he actually believes in what he's pitching, not just that he's moving inventory.

Deeper Dive

The most revealing moment in the interview is Reeder's story about someone pitching him a $5 million non-commercial drama with no recognizable cast. His response: "You should make this for $500,000—that way you'll only lose $500,000." It's harsh, but it captures his core frustration. Filmmakers aren't being realistic about the market-to-budget alignment because they've never looked at the actual data. The assumption is that if you make a good movie, distribution will follow. The reality, demonstrated across fifteen years of acquisitions work, is that most completed independent films get no meaningful distribution at all, and the ones that do were designed (in terms of cast, genre, budget, tone) to be pickupable.

Reeder's honesty about expectation-setting is where the interview gets uncomfortable but instructive. He notes that filmmakers from outside the industry often fall into two camps: those humble enough to be educated (easier to work with) and those who've succeeded elsewhere and assume business is business (harder, because they think they know better). He's learned to be direct about the Netflix question—it's almost always a long shot now—because false hope doesn't serve anyone. But he also keeps the door open to adjacent opportunities: theatrical, SVOD windows, hybrid self-distribution models, territory-by-territory sales. The point isn't that indie films can't make money; it's that the money rarely flows through the fantasy channels filmmakers imagine.

The film market section reveals something deeper about how information and relationships work in a closed industry. Reeder describes markets as swap meets where everyone knows everyone, where competitive intelligence is traded openly between non-competing sellers, and where a filmmaker with a badge and a goal can gather months' worth of knowledge in a few days. But it requires respect for the ecosystem—you don't interrupt a buyer meeting on day one; you collect business cards, follow up after the market, and build relationships over time. This is systems thinking applied to an actual industry; it's about understanding incentives, timing, and how communities actually function beneath the surface noise.

"You wouldn't take off in an airplane if you didn't know where you were going to land, right? So a movie is kind of like that. You don't want to jump in a plane and pack the plane up and get everybody up in the air and then say, 'Oh, where are we going to land? We don't know.' So having someone a producers rep like myself or a consultant on to help you... let me give you some ways to make sure you have, you know, landing gear and everything that you need to survive and do the best you can with your film."
videos.forYou: Reeder is making a systems argument about information and incentives in a closed industry—why filmmakers make decisions in isolation (low visibility into actual deal data), what buyers actually respond to (cast, not story), and how to read institutional spaces like film markets (timing, respect for gatekeepers, relationship-building over time). If you think about how institutions work and why people inside them are often blind to real constraints outside their immediate circle, this is a sharp case study. The video is valuable specifically for the frame it builds around distribution as a planning problem (solved early) versus a sales problem (unsolvable late). Skip the festival discovery myth; the insight is in understanding what information asymmetries actually limit independent filmmakers' choices.

videos.title: How to Write Memorable Characters — Yann Martel
videos.summary:
In this conversation with David Perell, novelist Yann Martel (author of Life of Pi and Beatrice and Virgil) shares a counterintuitive approach to character creation that inverts much of what creative writing instruction typically emphasizes. Rather than building complex, distinctive personalities or spending narrative energy on physical description, Martel argues for ordinary characters thrust into extraordinary circumstances—a compositional choice that fundamentally shifts what readers are willing to feel and how they engage with a story.

This video is valuable for anyone thinking seriously about craft, voice, and the relationship between form and reader psychology. Martel's reasoning cuts against optimization and surface-level character building, offering instead a philosophy grounded in how human attention actually works: we all believe ourselves to be normal, so the most powerful fiction holds up a mirror to the extraordinariness of life itself, not to invented personality quirks.

Key Takeaways

  • Martel deliberately does not focus on making characters distinctive or likable; instead, he prioritizes placing ordinary, even banal characters into extraordinary circumstances, which creates more resonance than the inverse approach of placing unusual characters into mundane situations.
  • The fundamental reason this works is psychological: because everyone believes themselves to be normal, ordinary characters function as vessels that allow readers to project themselves into the story and witness the extraordinariness of life happening to someone like them.
  • Physical description of characters through prose is an ineffective tool because words are poor at subtlety—emphasizing any single feature (like a nose) causes readers to wildly overestimate its prominence and turns a trait into a defining character marker rather than a realistic detail.
  • Martel explicitly rejects the common craft instruction to develop character texture and reader attachment through personality traits, emotional depth, or visual distinctiveness, calling this effort pointless compared to the power of circumstance and situation.
  • The extraordinary circumstances Martel employs are often catastrophic: a shipwreck in Life of Pi, the Holocaust in Beatrice and Virgil—but the characters facing them are symbolically and behaviorally minimal, wanting only ordinary things like three meals a day.
  • The compositional strategy relies on the principle that form shapes reader permission: by starting with an ordinary protagonist, the reader's defenses lower and they become willing to sit with ambiguity, difficulty, and the profound in ways they wouldn't if the character had already been marked as "special" or complex.
  • This approach suggests that character voice and emotional resonance emerge not from invention or personality design but from the collision between a human presence and an unforgiving reality, a dynamic that works because it mirrors how humans actually discover who they are.

Deeper Dive

Martel's argument is a direct challenge to a dominant strand in contemporary character writing: the assumption that making a character memorable requires building distinctive traits, quirks, or a rich interior life. His philosophy inverts this. By creating characters who are deliberately unremarkable—ordinary in their desires, their personalities, their preoccupations—he creates a psychological opening for the reader. The reader doesn't arrive at the story already enchanted by a personality; instead, the reader arrives as themselves, and then watches an ordinary human (not so different from them) confront something that shatters the normal world. This is more powerful than the reverse because it resonates with how humans actually experience life: we are all, in our own minds, completely normal people to whom extraordinary things happen.

The second insight is about the limits of language itself. Martel is explicit that words are "terrible at description," and this isn't a poetic exaggeration—it's a technical observation about how prose works. If you write "he had a prominent nose," the reader doesn't picture a mildly prominent nose; they picture an enormous, character-defining nose. The medium distorts. Rather than fight this distortion by trying to subtly describe appearance, Martel abandons the effort entirely. He doesn't describe his characters. This frees him (and his reader) from the false precision of visual detail and allows the character to remain a vessel—a point of consciousness through which the reader experiences the world. This is not laziness; it's a deliberate compositional choice about what matters in prose versus what matters in visual media.

The third layer is about what actually creates reader investment. Martel is suggesting that readers care most when they see themselves reflected in the protagonist, not when the protagonist is maximally interesting. The extraordinary circumstances do the heavy lifting of narrative and emotional intensity. The ordinary character ensures the reader stays present, stays identified with the protagonist, and remains open to the full weight of what's happening. It's a form of restraint: trust the situation to be powerful enough; don't gild the character with invented complexity that might actually distance the reader.

"I'd rather have an ordinary character in the face of extraordinary circumstances. And Pi's a perfect example of that. I never thought about Pi. I want someone who's interested in the world, who's open, and then things happen to him. Rather than someone who's really weird and curious and the banal happens."
videos.forYou: Martel's approach is fundamentally about restraint and form. He argues that ordinary characters work better than complex ones because readers see themselves reflected, not because distinctiveness is impossible. The real move is recognizing that language is a terrible tool for visual description—so stop trying—and that character resonance comes from the collision between a human presence and extraordinary circumstance, not from invented personality traits. If you think about composition and voice as structural problems rather than personality problems, this conversation challenges a core assumption about how to make readers care.

videos.title: Animal Characters Let You Say the Hardest Things — Yann Martel
videos.summary:
In this conversation with David Perell, author Yann Martel explores why animal characters are such powerful storytelling tools—and why they allow writers to communicate ideas and emotions that human characters often cannot. Martel has made this choice central to his most famous work, Life of Pi, and here he articulates the craft reasoning behind it. The insight goes beyond children's literature; it touches on how art sidesteps the prejudices and cynicism we carry into our engagement with human narratives, and how that suspension of disbelief creates space for harder truths to land.

Key Takeaways

  • Animals work as characters because most people like them—whether domestic or wild—in a way that transcends the cultural and religious prejudices we reflexively apply to human characters.
  • People project onto animals without the same cynicism they bring to humans; we rarely judge a rhinoceros or giraffe the way we judge a person from Texas or France, allowing the storyteller more freedom.
  • Animal characters create immediate suspension of disbelief—as soon as an animal enters the narrative, readers accept the fictional contract more readily than they do with human characters.
  • The plasticity of animal form and symbolism is enormous; animals come in radically different shapes, sizes, and behaviors, giving writers a wider palette to work with than human character archetypes alone.
  • Cynicism about animals is largely subjective attribution—we call hyenas cowardly and lions majestic, but these are human projections onto creatures that simply exist as they are.
  • Using animals artfully in storytelling allows writers to address difficult themes and uncomfortable truths because the reader's defenses are lower; the indirection of animal form creates psychological permission.
  • Children's literature has long understood this principle, but the technique works equally well in adult literary fiction when deployed with craft and intention rather than as a gimmick.

Deeper Dive

Martel's argument rests on a psychological insight: humans are tribal creatures loaded with prejudices that simplify life but also distort reality and create cruelty. When we encounter a human character described as being from a particular place or faith, we activate all those prejudices instantly. But when we encounter an animal character, that triggering mechanism largely shuts down. We're not cynical about animals because we don't have the same social and historical baggage invested in them. This creates a space where a writer can explore moral complexity, spiritual questions, survival, desperation, and transformation without the reader's defenses going up immediately.

What makes this insight particularly sharp is that it's not about deception or sleight of hand. The animal character isn't a disguise for a human idea; it's a genuine shift in how the reader engages with narrative. Once an animal enters the story, Martel observes, people "suspend their disbelief and are willing to believe it" far more readily than they would a human character in the same scenario. This isn't about intelligence or believability in the conventional sense—it's about permission. The animal form gives the reader implicit permission to sit with ambiguity, with faith, with survival stripped of sociological baggage. The emotional and spiritual questions Martel wanted to explore in Life of Pi—faith, terror, loneliness, the will to live, the animal nature of consciousness—could only happen in that cleared psychological space.

Martel also notes the formal plasticity of animal characters: they come in such radically different forms—shapes, sizes, temperaments, sensory worlds—that they offer a wider range of narrative possibilities than the familiar human character types writers typically work with. A tiger is not just a character; it's a viewpoint into consciousness itself, a way of asking what survival and self-preservation look like when stripped of human rationalization. This speaks directly to craft at the level of composition: choosing the right vessel for an idea is as important as the idea itself, and sometimes the vessel that lets an idea breathe is not human at all.

People like animals, either domesticated animals...or wild animals. People love wild animals because we hold them to be beautiful and pristine and something perfect where it's where we've sullied our existence, our globe with who we are...Whereas people are rarely cynical about animals. We tend to project onto them...You can do a lot with an animal character.
videos.forYou: Martel's arguing that animal characters work because they bypass the prejudices and cynicism that make readers defensive about human narratives—they create psychological permission to sit with ambiguity and difficulty. If you think about voice and composition as craft problems (not just output), this is worth the thirty seconds to the moment where he explains why suspension of disbelief kicks in differently with animals than humans. The insight isn't about gimmick or metaphor; it's about how form shapes what a reader is willing to feel, which is a sharper conversation about compositional choice than most craft talks get into.

videos.title: What Really Won the Trillion-Dollar Supreme Court Case | Neal Kumar Katyal | TED
videos.summary:
In November 2025, Supreme Court litigator Neal Kumar Katyal was tasked with doing what no lawyer had accomplished in 237 years: convincing the Supreme Court to strike down a sitting president's signature initiative—a four-trillion-dollar tariff regime imposed by executive order. The case hinged on whether presidential emergency powers could override constitutional checks and balances, and legal scholars said it was impossible. Katyal won a 6–3 decision. This talk is not a victory lap; instead, Katyal reveals the specific human and technological tools—four coaches and one AI system—that transformed his preparation and courtroom performance, and what that process teaches about the irreducible role of human connection in high-stakes persuasion.

Key Takeaways

  • Imposter syndrome is independent of past success; Katyal had won 52 cases and defended the Voting Rights Act, yet still heard the voice that said he didn't belong in that courtroom, and had to address it directly rather than ignore it.
  • Reframing fear as opportunity—shifting from "I have to do a great job" to "I get to defend the Constitution"—transformed terror into joy without eliminating the intensity, using nothing more than a change in grammar.
  • An AI system trained on 25 years of Supreme Court questions, opinions, concurrences, dissents, and separate opinions could predict not just which justice would ask which question, but the philosophical escape route the Chief Justice could use to vote with the majority while protecting the Court's institutional integrity.
  • Pattern recognition and predictability in judges is not a weakness to exploit—it is integrity and character made visible; Katyal's team treated AI predictions as a sparring partner, never as gospel.
  • Improv training—the discipline of actually listening and building on what someone has said rather than executing pre-planned arguments—flipped the energy in the courtroom from interrogation to dialogue.
  • The core irreplaceable human skill in an AI-augmented world is connection: the ability to look at another person, understand their worry beneath the surface, and adjust not just argument but delivery, tone, and pause to reach them.
  • When Justice Barrett asked a question the AI had not predicted, Katyal did something no algorithm can do: he really looked at her, understood her specific concern, and answered the worry rather than the question as asked.
  • At the highest stakes, Katyal brought no mountain of legal notes—only an email about connection and his family's names in his own handwriting, anchoring himself to the people he was fighting for.

Deeper Dive

What makes Katyal's account compelling is not the courtroom victory itself, but the granular honesty about how he built the mental and technical infrastructure to perform under the most extreme pressure. The four coaches address distinct failure modes: Ben (a sports psychology coach) tackled the internal narrative of doubt; Harvey (an AI system) provided foresight by pattern-matching across massive case law; Liz (an improv coach) trained Katyal to listen in real time rather than deliver pre-scripted arguments; and Bob (a meditation coach) provided the stillness and clarity to think clearly when the room was loudest. Each one appears to solve a different problem, but Katyal's framing—that each was a step toward connection—reveals the deeper architecture: connection to yourself (Ben and Bob), connection to information and pattern (Harvey), and connection to the other person in the room (Liz).

The most surprising element is the role of AI. Katyal is explicit that he did not blindly follow Harvey's predictions. Instead, the AI functioned as a relentless sparring partner that revealed patterns Katyal's team could then own, adapt, and deliver with human judgment intact. When Justice Barrett's question arrived unpredicted, the system did not fail; rather, the training in listening and adaptation—which Harvey had enabled, not replaced—allowed Katyal to respond authentically. This sidesteps both the hype (AI will do everything) and the fear (AI will replace humans); instead, it shows a specific workflow where prediction and pattern-matching free up the human practitioner to focus on the one thing only humans can do: actually connect with the person across from them.

The deeper insight is about what AI actually changes in high-skill domains. For centuries, the expert's edge came from reading the most, remembering the most, seeing the most—accumulated breadth and pattern recognition. AI erodes that edge almost completely. But Katyal's story suggests that the erosion creates a vacuum where the truly irreplaceable skill—persuasion grounded in empathy, presence, and the ability to adjust in real time based on what you actually perceive in the other person—becomes the only leverage left. He walks into court with an AI that has predicted the contours of the argument, coaches who have trained his body and mind, and then does the one thing the AI cannot: he looks at Justice Barrett and actually listens.

"You don't just got to do it, you get to do it, in an interview, in a negotiation, in a conversation that could save a marriage or end one. Any place in which you need to reach another human and actually connect. The question AI poses to every one of us is not will you be replaced? The question is, what is the irreducibly human thing that you do? Go deeper into it. Not to 'survive AI,' but to come home to yourself. That's where your edge lives."
videos.forYou: Katyal built a system where AI handled pattern prediction across decades of case law—freeing him to focus entirely on the one thing algorithms cannot do: read the room and adjust in real time. The story cuts against both techno-optimism and AI anxiety by showing a concrete workflow where a tool amplifies human judgment rather than replacing it. If you're thinking about where LLMs and agent-style systems actually land in real creative work, his honest account of how he used Harvey as a sparring partner—never as gospel—is sharper than most takes you'll hear. The other insight worth holding: when the moment arrived that the AI didn't predict, the training in listening and presence (not the AI itself) is what won.

videos.title: Yes, Winnie the Moo *is* in this TED Talk #TEDTalks @mayahiga
videos.summary:
Maya Higa runs Alveus Sanctuary, a virtual education center that livestreams rescued animals 24/7 to millions of people worldwide. Rather than operating as a traditional public zoo, Alveus exists almost entirely online — with 36 cameras capturing dozens of rescued animals at any moment, building a distributed community of people learning about conservation, animal welfare, and habitat protection. Since 2019, Higa has raised over 7.5 million dollars for global conservation causes through this model, proving that digital platforms can create genuine, scale-able engagement with wildlife and environmental education.

This talk matters because it demonstrates a concrete alternative to how institutions typically connect people with nature and conservation work. Instead of relying on foot traffic and physical visits — which stress animals and create unpredictable operational chaos — Alveus uses persistent, ambient livestreaming to let animals live naturally while humans observe and learn at scale. It's a case study in how the internet, used thoughtfully, can solve real institutional problems while creating something more sustainable and humane.

Key Takeaways

  • Alveus Sanctuary rescues animals from the pet trade, fur trade, and commercial agriculture operations, then uses them as ambassadors to teach millions of people about the systems that exploit wildlife.
  • The sanctuary operates primarily through 36 continuous livestreams rather than public visits, allowing animals to live in lower-stress environments while still reaching a global audience.
  • Opa and Momo are tiny primates rescued after being sold as pets online; they now educate viewers about the illegal exotic pet trade and the importance of rainforest conservation.
  • Finn the red fox and his companion Reed teach about the fur trade by living openly at the sanctuary, making the ethical cost of fur production tangible to viewers.
  • Winnie the Moo — a cow rescued from a beef operation in Oklahoma — represents Alveus's commitment to teaching about industrial agriculture's impact on both animal welfare and planetary health.
  • The livestream model has generated over 7.5 million dollars in conservation funding since 2019, proving that digital engagement can translate directly into real-world impact.
  • By removing unpredictable public foot traffic, the sanctuary reduces animal stress while paradoxically reaching far more people than a traditional facility ever could.
  • Higa's explicit mission is to use the internet to build the next generation of conservationists, treating digital platforms not as entertainment channels but as education infrastructure.

Deeper Dive

What makes Alveus distinctive isn't just that it livestreams animals — it's that Higa has designed the entire operation around a specific constraint: making the animal experience come first, and using that as the foundation for everything else. Traditional zoos and sanctuaries face a tension between visitor experience and animal welfare. Alveus sidesteps this by removing live visitors entirely. The animals don't perform; they simply exist, 24 hours a day, in front of cameras. This is a systems-level solution to a problem most institutions try to manage culturally or operationally. The insight is structural: sometimes the right answer isn't "do the same thing but better" but "remove the contradiction altogether."

The funding model is equally instructive. Most conservation organizations rely on grants, donors, and ticket sales — funding streams that create perverse incentives (maximize visitors, compete for donor attention, chase grant cycles). Alveus generates revenue through direct audience engagement and donations from people who are already present in the livestream. This aligns incentives: the more genuinely interesting and educational the content, the larger the community, the more funding flows to conservation. There's no tension between "keeping animals happy" and "making money." The two move together.

Perhaps most striking is the scale and durability of the model. Higa mentions millions of viewers and 7.5 million dollars in conservation funding — numbers that would take traditional facilities decades to achieve, if ever. Digital distribution lets a single sanctuary's impact scale without proportionally scaling operational complexity or land use. A cow rescued in Oklahoma teaches people across continents. This is infrastructure, not charity.

"My mission is to use the internet to build our next generation of conservationists. I think it's working."
videos.forYou: This is a straightforward case of someone shipping real infrastructure that solves a genuine institutional problem — removing the contradiction between animal welfare and public engagement by going fully digital. Higa's not talking about theory; she's built a system that generates millions in conservation funding and reaches millions of viewers simultaneously, and she's done it by designing at the constraint level rather than adding more processes on top. Worth watching if you're interested in how thoughtful use of technology can restructure an entire field's incentives.

videos.title: Writing A Damaged Main Character - Ron Mita
videos.summary:
Ron Mita is a screenwriter with produced credits including Robots, S.W.A.T., and 24 Hours to Live, who has also taught screenwriting for over two decades. In this conversation with Film Courage, he explores the craft of building damaged, flawed main characters—ones that feel real and compelling rather than archetypal—and explains his philosophy on when damage works, how it can tip into cliché, and why audiences connect with characters who are broken in recognizable ways.

Key Takeaways

  • Damage and flaws make characters more interesting than perfect heroes, but the key is avoiding the cliché of the damaged-guy-seeking-revenge by adding unexpected dimensions—like Indiana Jones's snake phobia, which revealed his vulnerability within the first 15 minutes and made him human.
  • When developing flawed protagonists, Mita starts with story and character fit, then asks "What if we twist this person?" rather than beginning with a damage profile; he keeps character bios to two or three pages and lets personality develop as the story unfolds.
  • Cowardice and reluctance can be as compelling as traditional damage; characters who don't want to be heroes but are forced into heroism (like Tom Hanks in Otto or the protagonist in Andy Weir's work) create tension and relatability without wallowing in trauma.
  • Hollywood treats female damaged characters differently than male ones—they tend to be beaten down more severely and require rescue by men, or they're paired with child-in-jeopardy tropes rather than given agency; Mita acknowledges this as a pattern he remains uncomfortable with and tries to avoid.
  • For ensemble casts wearing uniforms (like SWAT teams or soldiers), distinctiveness comes from small personal details—hobbies, hopes, dreams, small fears—that help casting directors find actors and make characters jump off the page.
  • Multi-dimensional characters need layered contradictions: an action hero who's afraid, a tough cop who's awkward in the classroom, or an assassin who realizes he wants to do one good thing before death—these layers reveal that the character is not a single note.
  • In 24 Hours to Live, Mita created a murderer audiences would cheer for by framing his final 24 hours as a chance at redemption rather than absolution; the character can't undo his past, but he can choose to end on doing the right thing, which studios found more commercially viable than a complete sacrifice.
  • Studio feedback and marketability matter; killing off a character completely can "unfranchise" a film, but allowing a door to redemption or return keeps stakeholders invested—a lesson Mita teaches his students as part of understanding how to write for both art and industry.

Deeper Dive

Mita's approach to damaged characters reveals a tension between artistic authenticity and commercial viability. He gravitates toward anti-heroes and flawed protagonists—citing Clint Eastwood's Dirty Harry as a formative influence—but he's acutely aware that pushing too far into darkness or dysfunction alienates studios and audiences. His solution is not to soften damage, but to make it specific and layered. He distinguishes between damage that is merely a sad backstory (childhood trauma, loss) and damage that shows up as contradiction in the present moment. Indiana Jones is fearless and brilliant, yet terrified of snakes. Dirty Harry is angry and brutal, but that anger came from losing a family. These specifics prevent damage from becoming a placeholder.

A striking moment in the conversation is Mita's candor about gender disparities in how screenwriters deploy damage. He notices that female characters often bear heavier emotional burdens and are positioned to need rescue, while male characters are allowed to be flawed and still heroic on their own terms. When he wrote a female-led thriller (Track Down), he avoided making her damaged, but felt compelled to give her a child in jeopardy—substituting one vulnerability trope for another. He doesn't pretend to have solved this problem, only that he's aware of it. This honesty about institutional bias within his own work is a form of craft awareness that many writers don't articulate.

The 24 Hours to Live case study reveals how studio feedback shapes character arcs in real time. Mita and his co-writers originally gave the protagonist a catastrophic death—blown to smithereens—believing this ultimate sacrifice would be cathartic. Studios rejected it. When they rewrote the ending to allow the character to simply die (but remain potentially resurrectable), the script sold the next day. Mita frames this not as compromise but as learning to write toward what studios will greenlight while keeping the core character work intact. The assassin still chooses redemption; he still dies doing the right thing. The only change was removing the finality. This reveals how economic incentives shape narrative structure, and how a writer can navigate that pressure without losing the character's integrity.

"I like when we see people because we're all damaged. And at some point maybe we all just want to say to everyone, 'Just go away. Leave me alone.' And it's nice to see that maybe someone else cares about you."
videos.forYou: Mita's core insight is that damaged characters work best when the damage shows up as contradiction in real time—not as backstory, but as behavior. The snake-phobic action hero, the cowardly everyman forced into heroism, the killer who realizes he wants to do one good thing before he dies. This specificity is what separates a compelling character from a cliché. The video also surfaces an uncomfortable institutional pattern: how damage is deployed differently for male and female characters, and how studios and writers police that difference. Worth watching if character voice and the gap between artistic intent and what actually gets made are things you think about.

videos.title: Should You Write With AI? — Yann Martel
videos.summary:
In this conversation between David Perell and acclaimed author Yann Martel, the discussion centers on a deceptively simple question: Should writers use AI in their creative process? Martel, best known for his novel Life of Pi, offers a refreshingly unambiguous answer—one that stands in sharp contrast to the prevailing discourse around AI-assisted writing. This video matters because it captures a working craftsperson's honest reasoning about the relationship between tool use and artistic integrity, framed not as ideology but as personal practice rooted in what makes writing worth doing in the first place.

Key Takeaways

  • Martel has never used AI for writing and sees no reason to start, comparing the idea to hiring someone else to do something fundamentally personal and generative.
  • His resistance isn't rooted in technophobia or puritanism—it's grounded in the simple fact that he loves writing itself, particularly the material and linguistic aspects of the craft.
  • For Martel, the creative language and compositional process is the point, not a means to an end, which makes delegating it to an AI tool a contradiction of purpose.
  • He explicitly predicts that AI-generated writing will become tired quickly, suggesting a natural cultural ceiling to its appeal beyond novelty.
  • His core argument is about the reader's experience: the value of artistic work lies in connection with another human being, not in the quality of the output alone.
  • Martel would rather read a poorly written short story by a human than a technically polished one generated by a computer, because the human effort carries irreducible meaning.
  • This stance reflects a distinction between writing as a tool for getting something done versus writing as the thing itself—a craft worth practicing for its own sake.

Deeper Dive

What makes Martel's position interesting isn't that he opposes AI—it's that he doesn't wrestle with the question at all. His answer has the clarity of someone for whom the answer was never actually in doubt. He's not saying AI tools are bad or that writers who use them are making a mistake. He's saying: this is not for me, because it violates what I actually care about in writing. That distinction is crucial. It's not a universal prescription; it's a personal boundary rooted in genuine preference, not fear.

The comparison to hiring someone else for an intimate act is revealing because it points to something rarely discussed in AI discourse: the role of *doing* in creative fulfillment. Martel doesn't see writing as a problem to be solved faster. He sees it as an activity worth preserving for its own sake. The materiality he mentions—the physical, linguistic, compositional act—is the reward, not the obstacle. This maps onto a larger conversation about what we value in art: is it the finished object, or is it the evidence of human intention and struggle embedded in that object?

His prediction that we'll "tire of that quickly" suggests he believes AI-generated text, however polished, will eventually reveal a sameness or lack of genuine voice that human readers will recognize and abandon. Whether that proves true depends partly on whether AI develops something we'd meaningfully call voice or intention—a question still genuinely open.

"I'd rather read a bad short story by a human than a good one by a computer, because after what you want is a connection. It's connection with another human being."
videos.forYou: Martel's position on AI and writing cuts against the optimization mindset—he's not interested in whether AI *can* write well, but in what's lost when the doing itself disappears. His argument is fundamentally about craft as practice rather than output, which lands in a specific place: if you care about the compositional act and the voice that emerges from it over time, delegating that act doesn't solve a problem, it removes the thing you're actually after. Worth listening for how he separates "good writing" (quality of prose) from "valuable writing" (connection with human intention).
